coredata.dc:title: Electronic properties of one-dimensional graphene quantum-dot arrays
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Pengfei
Tian, Wen
Zeng, Yongchang
Zhang, Zhenhua
Zhang, Junjun
coredata.dc:description: Using the first-principles method based on density functional theory, the electronic properties of various one-dimensional graphene quantum-dot arrays (1D GQDAs) are systematically studied. It shows that arrays present semiconducting behaviors when their edge structure is armchair-type, however, if their edge structure is zigzag-type, arrays are either metallic or semiconducting depending on the type of edge units: AA-type or AB-type. Punching nanoholes in quantum-dots would lead to an increase of the band gap for semiconducting arrays, but does not change a metallicity significantly for metallic arrays. Moreover, we find that the band gap of 1D semiconducting GQDAs decreases oscillatorily with size increasing, which means that the bandgap size is closely related to the quantum confinement and size effects. Our studies show that constructing various kinds of 1D GQDAs can effectively regulate the electronic behaviors of the graphene structure and obtain abundant electrical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: One-dimensional graphene quantum-dot array
Electronic property
Bandgap
First-principles method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One dimensional graphene quantum-dot arrays. (a) 1D QDAs derived from ZGNRs (W =10), the dotted box indicates the quantum-dot (unit cell). (b) 1D QDAs derived from AGNRs (W =15), (c) and (d) indicate the quantum-dots which come from punching nanoholes in the eight quantum-dots as shown in (a) and (b), and the nanoholes shape is highlighted with red lines. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Band structures (a) and transmission spectra (b) of the perfect ZGNR (W =10) and its derived 1D GQDAs. ZT, ZH, and ZD indicate 1D GQDAs with triangle-, hexagon- and diamond-shaped quantum-dots, respectively, while ZT1 and ZT2 are two 1D GQDAs with triangle-shaped quantum-dot, but having different edge structures.

Band structures (a) and transmission spectra (b) of the perfect AGN (W =15) and its derived 1D GQDAs. AT, AH, and AD indicate 1D GQDAs with triangle-, hexagon- and diamond-shaped quantum-dot, respectively, while AT1 and AT2 represent two 1D GQDAs with triangle-shaped quantum-dot, but having different edge structures.
Band structures and transmission spectra of 1D GQDAs punched with nanoholes. (a) and (b) For ZT1(H), ZT2(H), ZH(H), and ZD(H). (c) and (d) For AT1(H), AT2(H), AH(H), and AD(H).
Different-size quantum-dots. (a) For armchair-edged triangular quantum-dots. (b) For zigzag-edged hexagonal quantum dots.

Band structures and transmission spectra of 1D GQDAs with different sizes. (a) and (b) For 1D different-size armchair-edged triangular GQDAs. (c) and (d) For 1D different-size zigzag-edged hexagonal GQDAs.

head.ce:title: Electronic properties of one-dimensional graphene quantum-dot arrays

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronic Science, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronic Science
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Calculations show that one-dimensional graphene quantum-dot arrays can present semiconducting behaviors when their edge structure is armchair-type, however, if their edge structure is zigzag-type, arrays are either metallic or semiconducting depending on the type of edge units: AA-type or AB-type.

Using the first-principles method based on density functional theory, the electronic properties of various one-dimensional graphene quantum-dot arrays (1D GQDAs) are systematically studied. It shows that arrays present semiconducting behaviors when their edge structure is armchair-type, however, if their edge structure is zigzag-type, arrays are either metallic or semiconducting depending on the type of edge units: AA-type or AB-type. Punching nanoholes in quantum-dots would lead to an increase of the band gap for semiconducting arrays, but does not change a metallicity significantly for metallic arrays. Moreover, we find that the band gap of 1D semiconducting GQDAs decreases oscillatorily with size increasing, which means that the bandgap size is closely related to the quantum confinement and size effects. Our studies show that constructing various kinds of 1D GQDAs can effectively regulate the electronic behaviors of the graphene structure and obtain abundant electrical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Since graphene, a single-layered two-dimensional crystal composed of sp 2 hybridized carbon atoms, was successfully fabricated in experiment [1], its unique properties in mechanics, electronics, and magnetics have attracted much attention. In particular, it is predicted that it would have promising applications in nanoelectronics based on its unusual electronic properties [2–6]. Two-dimensional graphene can be tailored into other shapes as well, such as one-dimensional graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) [7], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [8,9], and graphene quantum rings (GQRs) [10–12], by using different techniques [13]. Among them, GNRs are the most popular structure investigated, and they have two typical families: zigzag-edged GNRs (ZGNRs) and armchair-edged GNRs (AGNRs), which can be achieved by cutting a two-dimensional graphene along different crystallographic directions. Due to different edge structures, ZGNRs always present metallic behaviors while AGNRs is semiconductor [14], if they are passivated by hydrogen atoms and neglecting the spin effects of electrons simultaneously.

Seeking for high-performance graphene-based devices is a challenging work. The key technique for graphene to be applied widely to nanoelectronic devices is how to make them have a suitable bandgap, especially for developing field effect transistors (FETs). However, two-dimensional graphene is a semimetal with a zero bandgap. Therefore, lots of methods, such as edge modification [15], heteroatom doping [16], introducing mechanical deformation [17] and topologic defects [18], are used to modulate the electronic structure of graphene, especially for a bandgap opening. Recently, an interesting approach proposed to engineering bandgaps is to punch periodic nanoholes on two-dimensional graphene or GNRs [19–21], which changes the electronic structure by forming inner edge structures to realize the transformation of the metal or semimetal to semiconductor. These studies indicate that the bandgap size of graphene or GNRs is closely related to the density of nanoholes punched, thus it is possible to effectively control their electronic structure by adjusting the density of nanoholes. It is worth noting that to obtain the favorable electronic properties of GNRs, except for introducing the well-defined inner edges and changing their shapes properly, an alternative pathway is to alter their outer edge structures, namely, introducing edge defects artificially. Due to the effects of edge states, it might be more effectively to regulate the electronic structure of nanoribbons significantly.

In this paper, we consider a special nanostructure, one-dimensional (1D) graphene quantum-dots arrays (GQDAs), which can be viewed as a long chain through connecting graphene nanoflakes with different geometrical shapes (triangular, hexagonal, and diamond-shaped) each other in some artificial way at one infinite line, or regarded as a special derivative of GNRs by tailoring edges of GNRs and introducing larger edge defects periodically. Relative to single graphene quantum-dot, the periodic potential is introduced when graphene nanoflakes are connected regularly into 1D GQDA, its electronic properties will be changed correspondingly. The calculated results show that due to the edge structure effect, size effect, and quantum confinement effect as compared with GNRs, 1D GQDAs could exhibit rich and unique electronic properties.

Fig. 1 shows 1D GQDA models in our studies, which can be formed by arranging numerous quantum-dots or cutting GNRs in a certain manner, as stated above. We use W to denote the width of GNRs, which, following convention, is defined as the number of carbon atoms chains along the ribbon width direction [16]. Therefore, W is the number of zigzag carbon chains for ZGNRs, while for AGNRs, W indicates the number of dimer carbon lines. For comparison to be convenient, in Fig. 1(a), we choose the ZGNR with the same width (W =10) to derive 1D GQDAs, and four different types of 1D GQDAs are formed. The black dotted box in figures indicates one quantum-dot (unit cell), which is one minimum repeated unit and used for the calculation of band structure. The first two kinds of quantum-dots are triangle-shaped, the difference of them is the edge structure: armchair type for one, while zigzag type for another, marked as ZT1 and ZT2 for corresponding 1D GQDAs, respectively, and using ZH and ZD denotes the arrays in which the quantum-dots are hexagon- and diamond-shaped, respectively. Fig. 1(b) shows four kinds of quantum-dots which are derived from AGNRs (W =15), each black dotted box represents a quantum-dot (unit cell), the corresponding 1D GQDAs come from the same shaped quantum-dots arranged periodically in one dimension (not shown here), marking them with AT1, AT2, AH, and AD, respectively. In addition, for further regulating the electronic structure of 1D GQDAs, we punch quantum-dots which are shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b), to form inner edge structures, as shown in Fig. 1(c) and (d), where the shape of hexagonal nanoholes is highlighted with red lines. ZT1(H), ZT2(H), ZH(H), ZD(H), AT1(H), AT2(H), AH(H), and AD(H) are employed respectively to indicate their 1D GQDAs. To avoid dangling bonds existing, all models studied are saturated with hydrogen atoms regardless of outer or inner edges.

We calculate the electrical properties of above mentioned models by using the first principles method based on density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in the Atomistix Tool-Kit (ATK) [17–23]. In order to solve the Kohn–Sham equation, the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) formulation of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) is used as the exchange–correlation functional, and employ Troullier–Martins norm-conserving pseudo-potentials to represent the atom core and linear combinations of atomic orbitals to expand the valence states of electrons. Considering the polarized effects of atoms, the single-zeta plus polarization (SZP) basis set is used for all atoms. In the Brillouin zone, the k-point sampling is chosen as 1×1×100, and 150 Ry is set as the cutoff energy for the grid integration, mainly controlling the size of the real space integral network partitioning and the solution of the Poisson equation.

Band structures for ZGNR-based 1D GQDAs as displayed in Fig. 1(a) and transmission spectrums of corresponding two-electrode devices constructed are computed, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), respectively. For purposes of comparison, the band structure and transmission spectrum for the perfect ZGNR (W =10) are also plotted together and marked as Z. From Fig. 2, it can be seen that the band structure and transmission spectra for 1D QDAs have a striking change as compared with those for the perfect ZGNR. Apart from ZT2, other three 1D QDAs present prominent semiconductor properties, with larger band gaps, which is completely different from the original metallic ZGNR. Although ZT2 still displays a metallic behavior, however, there is a great change occurring in the band structure and transmission spectrum, eight subbands exist densely near Fermi level in the region [−0.1,0.1]eV, which are partially shown in the inset, however, no any subband exists in the energy region [−0.1,−1.3]eV and [0.1,1.3]eV, and the transmission peak at the Fermi level in the perfect ZGNR is split into two transmission peaks in ZT2, but with much smaller values than the former.

As mentioned earlier, the edge structure of GNRs plays an important role to determine its electronic properties. After the GNRs are tailored into 1-D GQDAs, the edge structure can also be divided into two types: zigzag- and armchair-edged. Carefully looking at the edge structure of four 1D GQDAs as exhibited in Fig. 1(a) and their band structures as shown in Fig. 2, one can conclude that for both ZT1 and ZD with an armchair-type edge structure, they present semiconductor properties unambiguously. However, for both ZT2 and ZH with the same zigzag-type edge structure, they manifest metallic and semiconducting properties, respectively. Here, the rule that GNRs with the zigzag-type edge structure must be a metal seems to be broken down. This interesting issue will be clarified up later on.

The geometrical structures for four 1D GQDAs derived from AGNRs are shown in Fig. 1(b), as stated above, their band structures and transmission spectra of corresponding two-electrode devices constructed are calculated and shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), respectively. To make a comparison, the band structure and transmission spectrum of the perfect AGNR (W =15) are also given in Fig. 3 and are denoted as A. As can be seen, the band structure and transmission spectra for its four derived 1D GQDAs are changed drastically. For instance, a number of subbands are localized in the proximity of the Fermi level in AT1, namely, there exist 7 subbands in the region [−0.1,0.1]eV, which are shown partially in the inset, but no subbands exist in the energy range [−0.1,−1.3]eV and [0.1,1.3]eV. Two transmission peaks can be found near the Fermi level, similarly to ZT2. While for AT2, AH, and AD, they still exhibit semiconductor properties, but band gaps have an obvious change. From Fig. 1(b), one can see that the edge structures of AT1, AH, and AD are zigzag-type, only AT2 is of armchair-type. Armchair-edged 1D GQDAs are a wide-gap semiconductor, whereas zigzag-edged 1D GQDAs can show either metallic or semiconducting characteristics, similarly to earlier findings.

Summarizing above results on electronic properties for eight kinds of 1D GQDAs, we can find that when their edge structures are armchair-edge, e.g. ZT1, ZD and AT2, the arrays must be to present semiconducting features, while for zigzag-edged 1D GQDAs, e.g., ZT2, ZH, AT1, AH, and AD, could present metallic or semiconducting characteristics, depending on the geometry shape of quantum-dots. For armchair-edged 1D GQDAs, they show semiconducting features, which is easy to be understood, because adjacent two carbon atoms (dimmer) in the outermost edge are of AB-type. However, how to understand metallic or semiconducting characteristics for zigzag-edged 1D GQDAs? By analysis, we can suggest a rule: if each vertex angle of a zigzag-edged quantum-dot is at an angle of 60°, then its outermost carbon atoms must be in the same sublattice (A sublattice) [24], and assuming to take one outermost edge of this quantum-dot as one unit, which is referred to as the edge unit, then adjacent edge units must satisfy AA-type, similarly to the case of ZGNRs whose outermost-edge carbon atoms are of AA-type, so that the corresponding 1D GQDAs are a metal. Moreover, if there exists one vertex angle in the zigzag-edged quantum-dot is at an angle of 120°, then outermost carbon atoms at the adjacent edges must lie in two different sublattices (A and B sublattices), therefore, their edge units are of AB-type if still taking its each outermost edge as one unit, similarly to the case of AGNRs whose outermost-edge adjacent two carbon atoms (dimmer) are of AB-type, so that the corresponding 1D GQDAs present a semiconductor. The essential reasons are that for 1D GQDAs with AA-type edge units, they have a magnetic ground state [24], which must be semiconducting because the most stable magnetic state is lacking the density of state (DOS) at the Fermi level [25]. In turn, their higher energy state (nonmagnetic state), which is being discussed in this work, must be metallic. In contrast, for 1D GQDAs with AB-type edge units, they have not the magnetism at any energy state [24], and thus the semiconductoring state is energetically more favorable, as presented in this work.

According the above rule, we can deduce that: (1) the electronic properties of 1D triangular GQDAs are determined entirely by the edge structure, namely, the 1D QDAs are metallic when their edge structure is of zigzag-type because their vertex angles are all 60°, with AA-type edge units, and the 1D QDAs are a semiconductor if their edge structures are of armchair-type. For example, zigzag-edged ZT2 and AT1 present metallic characteristics, and armchair-edged ZT1 and AT2 display semiconducting properties. (2) 1D hexagonal GQDAs are always semiconductoring regardless of edge structure. For 1D zigzag-edged hexagonal GQDAs whose vertex angles are all 120°, with AB-type edge units, thus AH and ZH show semiconducting properties. While 1D armchair-edged hexagonal GQDAs are a semiconductor undoubtedly (not shown here) because their outermost-edge dimmer carbon atoms are of AB-type, as stated above. (3)1D diamond-shaped GQDAs exhibit semiconducting features independent of their edge structures, because even for zigzag-edged diamond-shaped QDAs, there also exist AB-type edge units for two sides of the vertex angle with 120°. Besides, we also find that for semiconducting1D GQDAs, the band gap of zigzag-edged GQDAs are much smaller than that of armchair-edged GQDAs, e.g., the band gap of the zigzag-edged AH and AD are much smaller than that of the armchair-edged AT2, and the band gap of the zigzag-edged ZH are smaller than that of both armchair-edged ZD and ZT1.

It is worth noting that the graphene nanoflake is a basic structure for various 1D GQDAs, thus electronic properties of arrays mainly depend on its edge structures and geometrical shapes unambiguously. However, the interconnecting and coupling manners between dots are also important, which can introduce different periodic potentials in 1D GQDAs and thus modulate electronic properties significantly.

We now turn to investigate effects of hexagonal-nanohole punched in quantum-dots. As mentioned before, eight kinds of punched1D GQDAs models ZT1(H), ZT (H), ZH(H), ZD(H), AT1(H), AT2(H), AH(H), and AD(H) are shown in Fig. 1(c) and (d), respectively. Their band structure and transmission spectrums of corresponding devices are drawn in Fig. 4 (a)–(d). Comparing them with Figs. 2(a) and 3(a), where the electronic structures of 1D GQDAs are free of nanoholes, it can be found that the semiconducting 1D GQDAs still remain to be semiconducting properties, but band gaps increase to a different degree. As mentioned earlier, the metallic ZT2 and AT1 gather a number of bands near the Fermi level, but no subbands exist in the energy range [−0.1,−1.3]eV and [0.1,1.3]eV. After punching nanoholes, the corresponding energy bands are shown in Fig. 4(a) and (c), marked as ZT2(H) and AT1 (H), as can be seen, the subbands in the vicinity of the Fermi level shift a little and appear at slightly higher and lower energy positions. In short, the behaviors of 1D GQDAs after punching nanoholes is that subbands move upward or downward from the center, resulting in the band gap increasing for semiconducting 1D GQDAs, but the metallicity almost remains unchanged for metallic 1D GQDAs.

In following, we discuss the size effects. Here, we select ZT1 and AH as representatives. In Fig. 5 (a), the different-size armchair-edged triangular quantum-dots are obtained just by adding a benzene ring at the each side each time, highlighted by various colors, and the corresponding 1D GQDAs are denoted as Ta, Tb, Tc, Td, and Te, respectively. In Fig. 5(b), the different-size zigzag-edged hexagonal quantum dots are achieved by increasing one zigzag carbon ring outward each time, as illustrated with various colors, and using Ha, Hb, Hc, and Hd to mark their 1D GQDAs, respectively. The electronic structure and the corresponding transmission spectrums of devices for these nine 1D GQDAs are displayed in Fig. 6 . It is very clear that they always keep semiconductoring properties with an increase of sizes, but the band gap decreases oscillatorily with size increasing, which means that the bandgap size is closely related to the quantum confinement and size effects. In addition, we find that all the band gaps of armchair-edged triangular 1D GQDAs are much larger than those of zigzag-edged hexagonal 1D GQDAs, which agrees with the band-gap regularity of semiconducting 1D GQDAs, as mentioned above, namely, the band gap of armchair-edged 1D GQDAs is always wider than that of zigzag-edged ones. Similarly, the size effect on the gap also can be found in the BN-embedded graphene [26].

Finally, we would like to point out that conventional DFT xc functionals used in our work might underestimate the band gap of 1D GQDAs, compared with other more exact algorithms, such as the GW method [26,27]. Therefore, a quasi-metal GQDA should be a narrow-gap one, and a narrow-gap GQDA should be a wide-gap one, etc. However, their essential regularity and changing trend will remain.

By using the first-principles method based on density functional theory, the electronic properties of 1D GQDAs are systematically studied. These quantum-dots are triangle-, hexagon-, and diamond-shaped, respectively. Calculated results show that when their edge structure is armchair-type, corresponding 1D GQDAs must present semiconducting properties. But when their edge structure is zigzag-type, the corresponding 1D GQDAs may be either metallic or semiconducting, depending on the geometry shape of quantum-dots. If each vertex angle of a zigzag-edged quantum-dot is at an angle of 60°, then its outermost carbon atoms must be in the same sublattice, and in turn the corresponding 1D GQDAs are metallic. Otherwise, zigzag-edged 1D GQDAs are semiconductoring. Punching nanoholes in quantum-dots would lead to an increase of the band gap for semiconducting arrays, but does not change a metallicity significantly for metallic arrays. Moreover, we find that the band gap of 1D semiconducting GQDAs decreases oscillatorily with size increasing, which is closely related to the quantum confinement and size effects. Our studies show that constructing various kinds of 1D GQDAs can effectively regulate the electronic behaviors of graphene structures and obtain abundant electrical properties. These findings might be able to contribute to develop graphene-based nanoelectronics devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 61371065, 61101009, 61201080, and 51302022), the Construct Program of the Key Discipline in Hunan Province, and Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province.
